title: Motor Cortex Cranial Windowing
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1577s","text":"This protocol describes the surgical procedure, instrumentation, and reagents necessary for the installation of a stabilizing headframe and cranial window over the motor cortex of an adult mouse. This protocol should be utilized for neural imaging and experimentation regarding the mouse motor cortex. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5sisr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8qdmj","text":"This procedure accommodates for 2 variations of stereotactic injections (Optional 1 and Optional 2). Specifically this protocol accommodates for injections before and after the craniotomy and durotomy. Timing of injections during cranial windowing surgeries is typically dependent on their coordinates. Injections outside of the cranial window should be performed prior to the craniotomy and durotomy to allow for adequate burr hole sealing. Injections within the cranial window should be performed after the craniotomy and durotomy to minimize cortical damage. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bgcfs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fqnrl","text":"Additionally, this protocol utilizes precision laser leveling, which involves a non-standard operation order where the headframe is leveled based off the craniotomy coverslip rather than the skull. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"cteu5","text":"Only perform this procedure in accordance with IACUC and veterinary requirements.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3o65s","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9dpt3","text":"This protocol has been written for review purposes only, and should not be utilized for training. Users of this protocol should be familiar with common vocabulary and technique for rodent neurosurgery.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"1rctc","text":"Reference protocol below for all general setup and takedown procedures for rodent neurosurgery:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"76bca","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"csomk","text":"Optional 1:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cn7ac","text":"For stereotactic injections prior to performing the craniotomy and durotomy, follow section titled with \"Optional 1\" in addition to the standard sections.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e5do7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fb0r1","text":"Optional 2, 2.1, 2.2:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1vmu0","text":"For stereotactic injections after performing the craniotomy and durotomy, follow sections titled with \"Optional 2\", \"Optional 2.1\" and \"Optional 2.2\" in addition to the standard sections. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4g3ca","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ebju","text":"This protocol utilizes precision laser leveling, which involves a non-standard operation order where the headframe is leveled based off the craniotomy coverslip rather than the skull. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"protocols","mutability":"IMMUTABLE","data":{"id":73620,"title":"General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery","title_html":"General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery","load_collapsed":true,"uri":"general-setup-and-takedown-procedures-for-rodent-n-cj5uuq6w","type_id":1,"image":{"source":"https://files.protocols.io/external/p5k5b8n2p-B2B270DB687411F08F960A58A9FEAC02.jpg","webp_source":null,"placeholder":"https://files.protocols.io/external/p5k5b8n2p-B2B270DB687411F08F960A58A9FEAC02.jpg","webp_placeholder":null},"guid":"94D06430759711ED8D35092646297B1E","creator":{"name":"Ali Williford","first_name":"","last_name":"","affiliation":"Allen Institute for Neural Dynamics","affiliation_url":null,"username":"ali--williford","link":"","user_image_file":{"guid":"00000000000000000000000000000001","file_name":"avatar.png","url":"https://www.protocols.io/img/avatars/001.png","mime":"image/png","size":0,"width":100,"height":100,"avg_color":"","scan_status":0,"created_at":0}},"stats":{"number_of_steps":105},"protocol_image_file":{"guid":"B2B270DB687411F08F960A58A9FEAC02","file_name":"p5k5b8n2p.jpg","url":"https://files.protocols.io/external/p5k5b8n2p-B2B270DB687411F08F960A58A9FEAC02.jpg","mime":"binary/octet-stream","size":203236,"width":0,"height":0,"avg_color":"","scan_status":0,"created_at":1753351087}}}}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"ati9","text":"Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) should be used at all times while operating this protocol.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d0rnl","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bmhpc","text":"Isoflurane Warning: Acute over-exposure to waste anesthetic gases (WAG) may cause eye irritation, headache, nausea, drowsiness or dizziness. Repeated exposure may cause damage to cardiovascular system and central nervous system. Refer to MSDS for additional information. Consult the surgical workstation guide to ensure all parts of the dispensation rig are functioning properly","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Remove Kwik-Cast well. Note any disturbances to the Vetbond seal and reseal if necessary.
Map diagram of protocol workflow.

After hair removal and disinfection, create midline incision with a scalpel blade from approximately behind the eyes to the front of the ears. Illustration of initial skin incision on mouse head. Mouse fixed to stereotactic instrument base via bite bar and ear bars. Fur from top of head has been removed and skin has been sterilized (see General Setup and Takedown Procedures).
Using Vanna scissors, cut a teardrop shape of skin away. Illustration of skin removal on mouse head.
On the left side, remove enough skin to expose 1-2mm of cheek muscle. On the right side, do not expose the cheek muscle. Anteriorly, stop 1-2mm anterior of the rostral rhinal vein.

Remove exposed periosteum by rubbing apart with cotton swabs and bunching near the edges of the skin. Then cut away with Vanna scissors at the skin edge. Use Artificial Cerebrospinal Fluid (ACSF) to rehydrate if necessary (i.e. periosteum dries out). Utilize a 10mL or 20mL syringe, 25G 5/8-inch needle, and a 0.2um syringe filter to store and dispense ACSF.

Detach 3mm of cheek muscle just posterior of left eye with tip of Dumont forceps. Do not remove more than 1mm deep into tissue. Be mindful not to damage the muscle structure of the eye. Cropped photo of mouse skull displaying extent of muscle removal.

Seal all along incision site with Vetbond. Use a Sugi Absorption Spear to absorb any excess fluid either prior or during. Extend Vetbond seal 1-2mm past incision site along healthy skin. Extending the vetbond seal over healthy tissue delays the formation of exudate. Additionally, unsealed soft tissue will weep fluid that can compromise the attachment of the headframe.
Locate Bregma and Lambda landmarks with Dovetail Clamp and Bregma Stylus, and use them to level the skull in the anterior-posterior axis within 0.1mm. Illustration of Bregma and Lambda landmarks.
If Lambda-Bregma offset is greater than 0.1mm in X, use the yaw adjustment on stereotactic alignment system to adjust the yaw to within 0.1 mm.
At midline, approximately midway between Bregma and Lambda, measure 2mm laterally on both the left and right hemisphere, ensuring that the skull is level in the medial-lateral axis within 0.15mm.

Optional 1​ This optional section accommodates injections prior to performing the craniotomy and durotomy. Typically, this is done for injections that will be outside the craniotomy window, as placement of the headframe will obstruct most coordinates. Injection coordinates within the craniotomy window are performed after the craniotomy and durotomy as it will minimize cortical damage. ​ ​ Skip this section if you will not be performing injections prior to the craniotomy and durotomy. go to step #18 .

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Injection by Nanoject Protocol V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via nanoject: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bp2l6nr7kgqe/v6 Begin at step 8.2.6 "Mark the Injection Site". Stop at step 8.5 "Suturing".

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Surgery for Delivery of Tracers by Iontophoresis V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via Iontophoresis: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.14egn8ewzg5d/v6 Begin at step 8.2.6 "Mark the Injection Site". Stop at step 8.5 "Suturing".

Once completed with the injection, fill burr hole with bone wax with the broken end of a cotton swab. Do not leave excess wax residue on the skull. Excess wax can contribute to an insufficient seal between the skull and the headframe.

Optional 2​ This optional section accommodates injections that will be performed after the craniotomy and durotomy. This is the first section out of three required. This first section is specifically for identifying and marking injection coordinates while the skull is level. These markings will be referenced in the second optional section (Optional 2.1).​ ​ Skip this section if you will not be marking injection coordinates for injections after the craniotomy and durotomy. go to step #18 .

Considerations: For injections within the cranial window, it is important to take the injection depth into consideration. Avoid targeting subcortical brain structures, as deep insertion of the pipette into the brain tissue can increase risk of edema. Additionally, maintain bregma as the fiducial point. Avoid the use of multiple viruses/pipettes as pipettes are not made identically (variance of up to 0.8mm difference). Any injection that includes depths deeper than 1mm as being normal to the skull, and depths that are exclusively at target depths of 1mm or less are normal to the cranial window. Furthermore, for the purposes of the shallower injections, "level" is approximately a roll of 16 degrees and a pitch of 0 degrees.

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Injection by Nanoject Protocol V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via nanoject: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bp2l6nr7kgqe/v6 Begin at step 8.2.6 "Mark the Injection Site". Stop before step 8.2.7 "Drilling the Burr Hole".

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Surgery for Delivery of Tracers by Iontophoresis V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via Iontophoresis: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.14egn8ewzg5d/v6 Begin at step 8.2.6 "Mark the Injection Site". Stop at step 8.5 "Suturing".
Ensure injection coordinate is marked as precisely as possible. This is best done with a fine point sharpie mark with a notch or point negative made from the marker pipette tip.
Create a temporary Kwik-Cast well around the craniotomy area. Cropped image of a Kwik-Cast well around the motor cortex craniotomy etch.

Dispense Kwik-Cast into an empty well within your well plate. Mix and let it sit for 00:01:00 to allow it to slightly stiffen. Kwik-Cast has an ideal working time within 2-4 minutes of mixing. As it solidifies, it will become increasingly stringy. Do not use once it becomes rubbery.

Once slightly stiff, use the broken end of a swab to create a temporary well around the craniotomy area. It will settle a little, so be sure to allow at least 1.5mm of space away from craniotomy coordinates and areas with excess fur. The temporary well needs to be larger than the craniotomy etch and should allow room to place and seal the coverslip.
Let Kwik-Cast set for about 00:05:00 before proceeding.

Optional 2.1​ This optional section accommodates injections that will be performed after the craniotomy and durotomy. This is the second section out of three required. This second section is specifically for aligning the injection device with the desired injection coordinates that were marked in Optional 2. Since the headframe has obscured the coordinate fiducials and a craniotomy will remove the coordinate markings, the injection device needs to be aligned to the precise X and Y (ML and AP) coordinates. After the craniotomy in Optional 2.2, the Z axis (DV) will be manipulated to perform the injections. If there are multiple coordinates, identify one of them and zero the coordinates creating that point as a fiducial to the remaining coordinates. Skip this section if you will not be marking injection coordinates for injections after the craniotomy and durotomy. go to step #32 .
Attach injection device with loaded pipette onto stereotactic arm and center over injection location. This stereotactic coordinate should be premarked from go to step #14 .
Place the injection device onto the stereotactic arm.
Zero the X and Y and coordinates over marked injection coordinate.
Remove injection device from stereotax arm.

DO NOT move the stereotactic arm out of the way. It will stay in this position during the craniotomy and durotomy. Nanoject/Iontophoresis pipettes should not be changed after setting up the stereotax arm. The variation in pipette straightness can cause the injection location to move up to 0.8mm.
Using a marker pipette or Bregma stylus attached to the stereotax arm, zero the pipette/stylus directly over Bregma.

Move the pipette/stylus 1mm anterior and 2mm lateral (left) of Bregma. Mark this coordinate with a sharpie or the tip of Dumont forceps on the skull. This will be the center of the window. Zero the pipette/stylus at this new coordinate.
The boundaries of the craniotomy are 1.5mm from the center of the window. Use a sharpie or the tip of Dumont forceps to mark 1.5mm anterior, posterior, left and right of the center of the window.
Remove 3mm coverslip from 70% Ethanol and rinse in two wells of ACSF.

Place the tracer coverslip over the mark for the center of the window and align the edges of the tracer coverslip with the markings made 1.5mm from the center of the window. Use a broken sterile swab in the probe holder to temporarily secure the tracer coverslip in place.
Using Dumont forceps or #11 Scalpel blade make an etch around the 3mm coverslip.
Remove swab and confirm etch with 3mm coverslip.
Using the drill with the FG1/4 or EF4, create a shallow trench over the etch.
Ensure trench is the correct diameter and shape by confirming with the 3mm coverslip.
Using the drill and the FG4, buff the 0.5mm around the outside of the trench.
Using the drill, remove any high points of Metabond that could cause a coverslip to break, or will cause the window to sit incorrectly (not level) within the craniotomy.

Using the drill and the FG1/4 or EF4 return to the trench and drill until the skull is cracked through all the way around. The drilling process will take 20-40 minutes. Rinse with ACSF to remove blood or debris from the well while drilling. Be aware that soaking the skull in ACSF will cause it to become soft and can cause the drill to take off more layers than anticipated.
Rinse the well using copious amounts of ACSF and sterile gauze/Kimwipes to get rid of debris before removing the skull island.

Place Hemostatic Agent Surgifoam within the well if anticipating bleeds. The visual cortex craniotomy contains the transverse sinus within the window. It is highly likely that you will require anticipatory Surgifoam ready within the window before removing the skull island.

Add ACSF to the well. Using Dumont forceps, gently pry up the skull at multiple points to ensure the skull island is sufficiently separated. If skull is still attached (i.e. bone bridge, not cracked), remove ACSF and continue drilling. This is especially crucial on the posterior edge. Rinse before removal.

When the skull island appears ready to be removed (sufficiently detached), use Dumont forceps to carefully remove the skull island, being careful to minimize pressure on the brain (i.e. pressure from skull island or tools).

Stop all bleeds with Hemostatic Agent Surgifoam, ACSF rinses and or sterile Sugi Absorbent Spears. Make sure there is always ACSF covering the brain surface. Ensure all bleeds are stopped as rapidly and efficiently as possible. Excess blood loss can result in mouse death.
Ensure brain is always covered with a layer of ACSF.
Make the initial incision using a durotomy probe.

Remove dura either by cutting along the bone line or using Dumont forceps to pull flaps over the bone line. Other than around the sinus or vessels connected to dura, no dura should remain within the window (unless connected to sinus or vessel).
Avoid tugging or pulling of the dura as this can cause vessel bleeds, sinus bleeds and/or "piano wire" lacerations.
Use caution around the sagittal and transverse sinuses.

Optional 2.2 This optional section accommodates injections that will be performed after the craniotomy and durotomy. This is the last section out of the three required. This second section is specifically for injecting the desired material at the injection coordinates that were marked in Optional 2. Skip to next section if not performing stereotactic injections within the cranial window. go to step #45 .
Ensure mouse's skull is level.
Return the mouse to the same angles as performed in go to step #8 .

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Injection by Nanoject Protocol V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via nanoject: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bp2l6nr7kgqe/v6 Begin at step 8.3 " If Injecting with Nanoject II" or 8.4 " If Injecting with Nanoject III". Stop at step 8.5 "Suturing".

Reference protocol Stereotaxic Surgery for Delivery of Tracers by Iontophoresis V.6 for performing stereotactic injections via Iontophoresis: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.14egn8ewzg5d/v6 Begin at step 8.4 "Inject Virus with Iontophoresis" Stop at step 8.5 "Suturing".
Raise and remove injection device from stereotax arm.
Rinse the desired coverslip within the two rinse wells of ACSF.

If there are visible air bubbles, rinse again and/or use the ACSF syringe or a sterile swab soaked in ACSF to detach bubbles. Air bubbles on the coverslip occasionally arise from surface tension when coverslip is being soaked and/or removed from ACSF well. This is to prevent the addition of air into/onto the brain.

Carefully place the coverslip into the craniotomy with Dumont forceps. If the coverslip is placed too forcefully or dropped, cortex damage may occur. It is best to have a significant amount of ACSF covering the cortex to cushion its placement.

Load the broken end of a sterile cotton swab into the probe holder and center it above the craniotomy. The end of the sterile swab should be as flat as possible to ensure even pressure while securing the coverslip. This can be done by cutting the end of the swab and hitting it against a flat and sterile surface. If accessible, a metal probe with a drop of silicone at the tip can be used as a substitute for a broken sterile swab.

Lower the broken side of the sterile cotton swab very gently onto the center of the coverslip to secure it in the craniotomy, being careful to not crack the coverslip. Excess pressure on the coverslip can cause detrimental brain compression.
Ideally, the 4mm edge of the coverslip should contact the full perimeter of the craniotomy. This may vary between mice due to skull shape and age.
Remove ACSF from the well with Kimwipes or Sugi Absorbent Spears until the surface tension of the ACSF is held between the 3mm and 4mm edge of the coverslips.

Seal coverslip by placing small drops of Vetbond using an insulin syringe around the coverslip via capillary action, forming a seal between the coverslip and skull surface. Let dry completely. Absorb extra ACSF with Sugi absorbent spears.
Carefully raise and detach the probe holder from the stereotactic arm.

Watch the Vetbond seal/coverslip closely to identify any ACSF leaks or seal breaks. If leaks occur, reattach and lower the probe, then reapply Vetbond. Proceed to next steps once complete seal is confirmed.
Secure the laser leveling tool in the stereotactic arm and turn laser on.
Align laser onto the coverslip and adjust the pitch and roll of mouse until the laser reflects back up to the center of the laser leveling tool.
Once laser leveled, place digital angle finder on the roll arm of the sterotax and zero. Roll the mouse to -16°.
Pitch the mouse down 3° using the stereotax markings.
Turn off the laser level and remove from stereotax arm.
Attach the dovetail clamp to the stereotax arm. Attach the motor cortex headframe to the clamp.

Lower the headframe until there is contact with the skull. Ensure that the headframe is not pushing down on the skull. The vetbond seal can often break during the placement of the headframe. Before proceeding to the next step, check for compromised areas and re-vetbond if necessary. Lowering the headframe too far down onto the mouse's skull can potentially crack a cranial suture line. If this occurs, your headframe will not attach to the skull due to Cerebrospinal fluid leakage.
Prepare and apply the first round of Metabond.

Apply Metabond inside the headframe and around the outside where the skull is easy accessible around the stereotactic frame. Place Metabond so that the majority of the skull within the well remains exposed. Let dry ( ~ 00:05:00 ).
Turn isoflurane off, remove earbars, and loosen the nose cone slightly.
Carefully detach the headframe from the earbar headclamp and/or earbars.
Prepare another Metabond tray.
Pull back nose cone completely, and remove the mouse from bite bar. Either lie the mouse on the heating pad or hold mouse in hand.

Apply Metabond to the outside of the headframe to cover all exposed muscle. Extend Metabond to 1-2mm past the exposed muscle and tissue to create a seal. Be as efficient and quick as possible to ensure mouse stays anesthetized during this step. Ensure Metabond is not left too close to eye and does not have any sharp points. This is to avoid any irritation to the mouse.

Using the 0° earbar headframe clamp, return mouse back onto the stereotax. Ensure headframe is secured in place by ear bar clamp. Be as efficient and quick as possible to ensure mouse stays anesthetized during this step.
Place headframe plate into the clamp, nose cone to nose, and turn isoflurane on.
Replace saran wrap over mouse body.
Remove mouse from stereotactic instrument base once Metabond is fully dry.
Turn off isoflurane.
Detach headframe clamp from headframe carefully.
Remove earbars.
Remove mouse teeth from bitebar by lightly scruffing mouse in a way that raises the teeth out of the bite bar.
Obtain the mouse’s postoperative weight.
Place the mouse back in a recovery cage and put the cage on the 37 °C heat plate.
Reference General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery protocol for takedown procedures. General Setup and Takedown Procedures for Rodent Neurosurgery by Avalon Amaya
creator: Conor Grasso
